root \ about \ projects + writings \ academic website

examples of discrete probability distributions

Table of contents

  1. A return to the big picture: filling the toolkit
  2. Bernoulli distributions
  3. Binomial distributions
  4. Geometric distributions
  5. Hypergeometric distributions
  6. Poisson distributions

1. A return to the big picture: filling the toolkit

I want to begin by returning to a discussion we had toward the end of the section on probability spaces.

One of the goals of applied mathematics is to use mathematical models to represent real-world scenarios. Of course, no real-world scenario comes packaged together with a preferred mathematical model. Nature or the Universe doesn’t somehow tell you which model to use—rather, it is up to you to choose what you believe is the most appropriate model.

Making a good choice requires lots of experience and theoretical knowledge, but it also requires you to have a wide knowledge of the various models that are out there. So, as you go along in your studies, you’ll want to build up a library of models that you know a lot about, so that when you’re confronted with a new scenario out in the real world, the chances are high that you know the perfect model. Think of it as “filling your toolkit.”

In this section, we will begin studying different families of probability distributions for discrete random variables. Think of these distributions as probabilistic mathematical models that you would be able to use later on, if you ever need them:

We will study five families of probability distributions in this section—you can see their names in the Table of Contents above. Later on, when we study continuous random variables, we will study even more families of probability distributions.

Notice that I referred to these probability distributions as belonging to families. Indeed, each of the five types of distributions that we will study contain infinitely many individual probability distributions. What distinguishes the distributions amongst each other in each family are different parameter settings. For example, in the second section below, we will learn that there is an individual Bernoulli probability distribution for each real number $p$ between $0$ and $1$ (inclusive). In technical jargon, we would therefore say that the Bernoulli distributions are parametrized by $p$.

Families of probability distributions may need more than one parameter in order to be completely specified. For example, as we will see, hypergeometric distributions actually require three parameters.

So, if you’re confronted with a real-world scenario, and you decide to model it using one of the five types of distributions that we learn about in this section, you would then also need to choose the correct parameter setting within your chosen type. You would need to “tune” the parameters, as we usually say.

My descriptions of the families of probability distributions will all follow the same pattern:

  1. I will begin by defining the distributions in abstract, purely mathematical terms. This includes a description of the parameters of the distributions and the range of the random variable, as well as formulas for the probability functions.

  2. I will show you several plots of probability histograms, to help convey how different parameter settings affect the shapes of the distributions.

  3. I will describe an archetypical real-world scenario in which the family of distributions naturally arises. These scenarios will help explain the abstract definitions.

  4. I will give you formulas for the expectations and variances.

  5. Interspersed between the first four steps are various comments, remarks, and miscellaneous facts, as well as Problem Prompts pointing to the worksheet.

In many introductory texts on probability and statistics, steps (1) and (3) are often inverted, with the description of an archetypical scenario coming before the abstract definition. I have elected to give the abstract definition first because I want to communicate the idea that these probability distributions exist completely on their own, and are independent of any real-world scenario in which they might be used. For example, the form of a hypergeometric distribution is often motivated by considering an urn filled with balls of two different colors (and I will use the same example!). But even though this real-world scenario nicely describes why a hypergeometric distribution looks the way it does, it might also give the false impression that these distributions are only used in scenarios involving urns and balls. But this isn’t the case! These distributions enjoy their own existence, independent of urns and balls.

In short: I want to encourage the reader to quickly begin thinking about mathematical models as abstract objects, just as a computer scientist would think of an algorithm as an abstract procedure independent of any particular implementation in computer code.

As you read through the sections below, you will notice that the probability spaces $S$ that serve as the domains of random variables $Y:S\to \bbr$ have all but disappeared. They haven’t completely gone away, of course, since whenever we talk about random variables they are there lurking in the background. But this is typical of a course at this level—the probability spaces recede, and random variables take their place as the main objects of study.

Ok! Enough talking! Let’s begin our exploration!

2. Bernoulli distributions

Our first family of distributions are certainly the simplest. But don’t let their simplicity fool you—we will see soon enough that they are very important, since they sometimes occur as the “building blocks” of more complicated and complex distributions!

Definition. Let $p$ be a real number with $0\leq p \leq 1$. A random variable $Y$ is said to have a Bernoulli distribution with parameter $p$, denoted

\begin{equation}\label{dist-eqn} Y \sim \Ber(p), \end{equation}

if it has range $\{0,1\}$ and its probability function $p(y)$ is given by

\begin{equation}\label{bern-eqn} p(y) = p^y(1-p)^{1-y}. \end{equation}

First, notice that the single letter “$p$” means different things on the two sides of the equation \eqref{bern-eqn}. On the left, it stands for the name of the probability function, while on the right, it stands for the parameter. I’m as tolerant of abuses and misuses of notation as the next guy, but this one seems pretty terrible to me. Nevertheless, because your book uses this notation, I feel compelled to use it as well so as not to confuse you. That’s how these horrible choices in notation—always hallowed by tradition—gain inertia of their own! And we’re powerless to stop them!

The notation \eqref{dist-eqn} is simply shorthand for: “The random variable $Y$ has a Bernoulli distribution with parameter $p$.” The symbol “$\sim$” simply means “is distributed as”. This is incredibly helpful notation and can be used in many ways to shorten the amount that we need to write.

The outputs of a Bernoulli random variable consist of just two values, $0$ and $1$. For that reason, Bernoulli random variables might also be called binary random variables.

Since $y$ can be only $0$ or $1$, this means that the formula \eqref{bern-eqn} for the probability function could equally be re-written as

\begin{equation}\label{bern2-eqn} p(y) = \begin{cases} p & : y=1, \\ 1-p & : y=0.\end{cases} \end{equation}

Therefore, a random variable $Y\sim \Ber(p)$ outputs the value $1$ with probability $p$, and the value $0$ with probability $1-p$. Using this fact, I bet you would have no problem drawing probability histograms for various settings for $p$. Here’s what you would get:

In what sort of real-world scenario would Bernoulli random variables naturally occur? Here’s one:

An archetypical Bernoulli scenario.

Q: Suppose that a coin is flipped once and $Y$ is the number of heads obtained. Suppose further that we allow for the possibility that the coin is loaded, so that it lands heads with probability $p$ (which may not be $0.5$!). What is the distribution of $Y$?

A: $Y\sim \Ber(p)$.

It’s pretty obvious that $Y$ is distributed as a Bernoulli random variable, right? After all, since $Y$ is the number of heads obtained after one flip, there are only two options: $0$ or $1$. Moreover, they are telling us that $Y=1$ occurs with probability $p$, and therefore $Y=0$ must occur with whatever probability is “left over,” which is exactly $1-p$. So, the probability function of $Y$ must be \eqref{bern2-eqn}, which means $Y\sim \Ber(p)$ by definition.

You might feel that this archetypical scenario is so simple that it borders on feeling trivial and contrived. I admit that I have that impression, too. But I want to include it if only to make our current discussion match our discussions of the more complicated distributions in the upcoming sections, in which the archetypical scenarios are definitely not so trivial.

Clearly, any scenario which results in a binary outcome with fixed probabilities may be modeled using a Bernoulli random variable. We will explore a few such scenarios in the worksheet problems when we get to the Problem Prompt below. But first, let’s state the expectations and variances of Bernoulli variables:

Expectations and variances of Bernoulli variables. If $Y\sim \Ber(p)$, then

\begin{equation}\notag E(Y) = p \quad \text{and} \quad V(Y) = p (1-p). \end{equation}

The proofs of these equalities are mere trivialities: For the expectation, we have

\begin{equation}\notag E(Y) = 0\cdot(1-p) + 1\cdot p = p, \end{equation}

while for the variance we have

\begin{equation}\notag V(Y) = E(Y^2) - E(Y)^2 = 0^2 \cdot (1-p) + 1^2 \cdot p - p^2 = p (1-p). \end{equation}

Notice, in particular, that the mean of a variable $Y\sim \Ber(p)$ is the parameter $p$. So, it is accurate to say that Bernoulli random variables are parametrized by their means. We will see that this is also true for a few other families of random variables besides the Bernoulli random variables.

Problem Prompt. Let’s finish off our (short) discussion of our first family of distributions with problems ??? on the worksheet.

3. Binomial distributions

Next come distributions whose probability functions look a lot like the probability functions of Bernoulli random variables. Notice that these distributions require two parameters to describe them.

Definition. Let $n\geq 0$ be an integer and let $p$ be a real number with $0\leq p \leq 1$. A random variable $Y$ is said to have a binomial distribution with parameters $n$ and $p$, denoted

\begin{equation}\notag Y \sim \Bin(n,p), \end{equation}

if it has range $\{0,1,\ldots,n\}$ and its probability function $p(y)$ is given by

\begin{equation}\notag p(y) = \binom{n}{y}p^y (1-p)^{n-y}. \end{equation}

Notice that a random variable $Y \sim \Bin(1,p)$ is nothing but a Bernoulli random variable! Thus, technically, the class of binomial random variables includes the class of Bernoulli ones.

Here are some probability histograms, for various settings of the parameters:

Increasing the $n$ parameter increases the size of the range of $Y\sim \Bin(n,p)$, which generally spreads out the distribution (increases its variance). Larger values of $p$ tend to push the mean of $Y$ toward the larger numbers in its range.

There is a very close connection between Bernoulli random variables and binomial ones, beyond the fact that $\Bin(1,p) = \Ber(p)$. This connection is displayed in the archetypical scenario I give immediately below, which anticipates a result I will prove a little later on that shows Bernoulli random variables can be used to “build” binomial random variables.

An archetypical binomial scenario.

Q: Suppose that a coin is flipped $n$ times and that each flip is independent of the others. Suppose further that we allow for the possibility that the coin is loaded, so that it lands heads with probability $p$ (which may not be $0.5$!). If $Y$ is the number of heads obtained, what is the distribution of $Y$?

A: $Y\sim \Bin(n,p)$.

The sample space $S$ on which $Y$ is defined consists of all $n$-tuples of the form

\begin{equation}\label{tuple-eqn} (H, T, H, H, T, \ldots, H, T), \end{equation}

which represent the result after flipping the coin $n$ times. If there are $y$ heads in this $n$-tuple, then the probability of obtaining this particular sequence is

\begin{equation}\notag P(\text{the particular sequence above}) = p^y(1-p)^{n-y}. \end{equation}

Indeed, this follows from the Product Rule for Probability, since the flips are independent of each other, and we obtain $H$ with probability $p$ and $T$ with probability $1-p$.

It follows that

\begin{equation}\notag P(Y=y) = k p^y(1-p)^{n-y}, \end{equation}

where $k$ is the total number of $n$-tuples \eqref{tuple-eqn} with $y$ heads. To compute $k$, we simply note that an $n$-tuple \eqref{tuple-eqn} with $y$ heads is uniquely specified by the (left-to-right) positions of the $H$’s. For example, in \eqref{tuple-eqn}, an $H$ occupies positions $1$, $3$, and $4$. So, to specify the $n$-tuple, from all possible $n$ positions we need to choose $y$ of them for the $H$’s. But there are exactly $\binom{n}{y}$ many ways to make these choices, and hence $k = \binom{n}{y}$, which is what we wanted to prove. Thus, $Y$ is indeed a $\Bin(n,p)$ random variable.

Beyond just flipping coins, binomial random variables may be used to model any scenario which consists of a finite sequence of repetitions of some scenario with independent binary outcomes with fixed probabilities. Such scenarios are even called binomial experiments in your textbook. We will explore additional scenarios in the Problem Prompt below.

Let’s now give the expectations and variances of binomial variables:

Expectations and variances of binomial variables. If $Y\sim \Bin(n,p)$, then

\begin{equation}\label{bin-exp-eqn} E(Y) = np \quad \text{and} \quad V(Y) = np (1-p). \end{equation}

The derivations of these formulas based only on the definitions are quite tedious (see the derivations in your textbook). A much more elegant method to obtain them goes through gadgets called moment-generating functions, which we will explore later. Thus, we will postpone the derivations till then.

From \eqref{bin-exp-eqn}, you can see that $E(Y)$ is jointly proportional to $n$ and $p$, so that increasing one (while holding the other fixed) increases the mean. The variance $V(Y)$ is also proportional to $n$, so that increasing $n$ (while holding $p$ fixed) also increases the spread of the distribution. These are confirmations of what we saw in the probability histograms above.

Before moving on to the Problem Prompt, I want to give a useful result that I alluded to above: Under certain circumstances, Bernoulli random variables may be used to “build” binomial random variables. These “certain circumstances” reach a bit beyond the theory that we currently know, since they refer to independence of random variables, which is a topic we haven’t discussed yet. This latter concept, in turn, relies upon joint distributions, which we also haven’t talked about yet. Nevertheless, these concepts are used so lightly that I believe the gist of the proof remains intelligible.

This being said, if you want to skip the proof and take the result on faith, that’s fine with me. Just be aware that independence of random variables will return in full force a little later on. In fact, this will be our first encounter with a so-called independent and identically distributed (IID) sequence of random variables, which will be very important in future sections.

Theorem (binomial variables as sums of Bernoulli variables). Let $Z_1,Z_2,\ldots,Z_n$ be a sequence of independent Bernoulli variables, all with the same distribution $\Ber(p)$. Then the random variable

\begin{equation}\notag Y = Z_1 + Z_2 + \cdots + Z_n \end{equation}

is a $\Bin(n,p)$ random variable.

To prove that $Y \sim \Bin(n,p)$, we need to prove that

\begin{equation}\notag P(Y=y) = \binom{n}{y} p^y (1-p)^{n-y}, \quad \text{for } y=0,1,\ldots,n. \end{equation}

However, we have

\begin{equation}\label{huh-eqn} P(Y=y) = P(Z_1 + \cdots + Z_n = y) = \sum_{z_1+\cdots + z_n=y} P(Z_1=z_1, \ Z_2=z_2, \ \ldots \ , Z_n = z_n), \end{equation}

where the sum runs over all numbers $z_1,\ldots,z_n$ such that $0\leq z_1,\ldots,z_n\leq 1$ and $z_1+\cdots + z_n = y$. The commas in the joint probability

\begin{equation}\label{joint-eqn} P(Z_1=z_1, \ Z_2=z_2, \ \ldots \ , Z_n = z_n) \end{equation}

represent the word “and,” so that this is the probability that

\begin{equation}\notag Z_1 = z_1, \quad \textbf{and} \quad Z_2 = z_2, \quad \textbf{and} \quad Z_3 = z_3, \quad \textbf{and} \quad \textit{etc}. \end{equation}

Now, since the Bernoulli variables $Z_k$ are independent, the probability \eqref{joint-eqn} factors as

\begin{equation}\notag P(Z_1=z_1, \ Z_2=z_2, \ \ldots \ , Z_n = z_n) = P(Z_1=z_1)P(Z_2=z_2) \cdots P(Z_n=z_n). \end{equation}

But each $Z_k\sim \Ber(p)$, so that, returning to \eqref{huh-eqn}, we get:

\begin{equation}\notag P(Y=y) = \sum_{z_1+\cdots + z_n=y} p^{z_1}(1-p)^{z_1} p^{z_2}(1-p)^{z_2} \cdots p^{z_n} (1-p)^{z_n} = \sum_{z_1+\cdots + z_n=y} p^y(1-p)^{n-y}. \end{equation}

But, given an integer $y$ with $0\leq y\leq n$, how many ways can we write $y=z_1+\cdots + z_n$ where each $z_k$ is either $0$ or $1$? Exactly $\binom{n}{y}$ many ways! Thus, we have

\begin{equation}\notag P(Y=y) = \binom{n}{y} p^y (1-p)^{n-y}, \end{equation}

as desired.

Problem Prompt.

4. Geometric distributions

The ranges of Bernoulli and binomial random variables are finite. Now, we turn our attention toward a class of random variables with countably infinite ranges:

Definition. Let $p$ be a real number with $0\leq p \leq 1$. A random variable $Y$ is said to have a geometric distribution with parameter $p$, denoted

\begin{equation}\notag Y \sim \Geo(p), \end{equation}

if it has range $\{1,2,\ldots\}$ and its probability function $p(y)$ is given by

\begin{equation}\notag p(y) = p (1-p)^{y-1}. \end{equation}

So, we’re back to distributions that are parametrized by a single parameter. For different settings of $p$, here are some probability histograms:

It appears that increasing $p$ decreases both the mean and the variance of the distribution. This will be confirmed below when we uncover formulas for $E(Y)$ and $V(Y)$ when $Y\sim \Geo(p)$.

Just like the scenarios for Bernoulli and binomial random variables in the previous two sections, our archetypical scenario for a geometric random variable will also involve coin flips. But pay close attention, because the random variable doesn’t count the number of heads any longer!

An archetypical geometric scenario.

Q: Suppose that a coin is flipped until heads is obtained. Suppose further that each flip is independent of the others, and that we allow for the possibility that the coin is loaded, so that it lands heads with probability $p$ (which may not be $0.5$!). If $Y$ is the number of flips it takes to obtain a head, what is the distribution of $Y$?

A: $Y\sim \Geo(p)$.

The sample space $S$ on which $Y$ is defined consists of all sequences of the form

\begin{align} &(H), \notag \\ &(T,H), \notag \\ &(T, T, H), \notag \\ &(T,T,T,H), \notag \\ &\textit{etc}.\notag \end{align}

The probability to obtain the first sequence is $p$; by independence, the probability to obtain the second is $p(1-p)$; the probability for the third is $p(1-p)^2$; and the probability for the fourth is $p(1-p)^3$. Do you see the pattern? If $y$ is the number of flips it takes to obtain a head, then we clearly have

\begin{equation}\notag P(Y=y) = p (1-p)^{y-1}, \end{equation}

so that $Y\sim \Geo(p)$.

Like Bernoulli and binomial random variables, geometric random variables do not only apply in scenarios that involve coin flips. Indeed, the coin flips in our archetypical scenario can represent the binary outcomes of any scenario, as long as they are independent (when performed one after another) and have fixed probabilities.

Expectations and variances of geometric variables. If $Y\sim \Geo(p)$, then

\begin{equation}\notag E(Y) = \frac{1}{p} \quad \text{and} \quad V(Y) = \frac{1-p}{p^2}. \end{equation}

As with the expectations and variances of binomial random variables, we will save the derivations of these formulas till we learn about moment-generating functions. But do notice that our intuition outlined above regarding the effect of $p$ on the shape of the distributions is confirmed by these formulas: Increasing $p$ decreases both the mean and the variance of $Y$.

Problem Prompt.

5. Hypergeometric distributions

The distributions in this section definitely have the most complicated expressions for their probability functions (they require three parameters!), but as we will see in our archetypical scenario, these distributions arise quite naturally.

Definition. Let $N\geq 0$ be an integer, and let $n$ and $r$ be integers with $0\leq r,n\leq N$. A random variable $Y$ is said to have a hypergeometric distribution with parameters $N$, $r$, and $n$, denoted

\begin{equation}\notag Y \sim \HGeo(N,r,n), \end{equation}

if it has range all $y$ such that

\begin{equation}\label{range-eqn} 0 \leq y \leq r \quad \text{and} \quad 0 \leq n-y \leq N-r \end{equation}

and its probability function $p(y)$ is given by

\begin{equation}\notag p(y) = \frac{\binom{r}{y}\binom{N-r}{n-y} }{\binom{N}{n}}. \end{equation}

I guarantee that this distribution will make much more sense when we study the model scenario below! So hold on for just a second!

These distributions have quite curious names—what exactly does hypergeometric mean? Here’s an enlightening explanation.

Here are some probability histograms for a hypergeometrically distributed random variable, for various settings of the parameters:

Comparing the distributions in rows, it appears that larger values of the $n$ parameter increase the mean. Comparing the distributions in columns, it appears that increasing the value of the $r$ parameter does the same, i.e., increases the mean.

Now, in what type of crazy scenario would something as complex and complicated as a hypergeometric distribution occur? Actually, such scenarios aren’t crazy at all!

An archetypical hypergeometric scenario.

Q: Suppose we have a collection of $N$ balls in an urn, with $r$ of them red, and the remaining $N-r$ black. Let $Y$ denote the number of red balls in a given random selection of $n$ balls from the urn. What is the distribution of $Y$?

A: $Y\sim \HGeo(N,r,n)$.

Here’s the justification. Since the selection is random, any selection is just as likely as any other, so

\begin{equation}\notag P(\text{single selection of $n$ balls}) = \frac{1}{\binom{N}{n}}, \end{equation}

because there are exactly $\binom{N}{n}$ ways to choose $n$ balls from a total of $N$. It then follows that

\begin{equation}\label{almost-eqn} P(Y=y) = k \times \frac{1}{\binom{N}{n}} = \frac{k}{\binom{N}{n}}, \end{equation}

where $k$ is the number of ways to choose $n$ balls from the urn, with $y$ of them red, and the remaining $n-y$ black. So, our goal is to compute $k$.

Well, if there are $r$ red balls, how many ways are there to choose $y$ of them? Exactly $\binom{r}{y}$ ways. And once these $y$ red balls are chosen, we would then need to choose $n-y$ black balls, of which there are exactly $\binom{N-r}{n-y}$ ways since there are $N-r$ black balls. So, by the Product Rule for Counting, we have

\begin{equation}\notag k = \binom{r}{y} \binom{N-r}{n-y}. \end{equation}

Therefore, from \eqref{almost-eqn} we get that

\begin{equation}\notag P(Y=y) = \frac{\binom{r}{y}\binom{N-r}{n-y} }{\binom{N}{n}}, \end{equation}

which is exactly what we wanted to show.

With this new knowledge, I encourage you to return to the probability histograms above and see if you can see why the $n$ and $r$ parameters affect the shapes of the distributions in the ways that they do. Also, notice that the range \eqref{range-eqn} now makes complete sense, since

\begin{equation}\notag 0 \leq y \leq r \end{equation}

says that you cannot have more red balls in your selection than the total number $r$ of red balls, while likewise

\begin{equation}\notag 0 \leq n-y \leq N-r \end{equation}

says that you cannot have more black balls in your selection than the total number $N-r$ of black balls.

Now, hold on to your hats, because here come the formulas for the expectations and variances of hypergeometric random variables:

Expectations and variances of hypergeometric variables. If $Y\sim \HGeo(N,r,n)$, then

\begin{equation}\notag E(Y) = \frac{nr}{N} \quad \text{and} \quad V(Y) = n \left( \frac{r}{N} \right) \left( \frac{N-r}{N} \right)\left( \frac{N-n}{n-1} \right). \end{equation}

The effect of the parameters on the mean is clear, since it is jointly proportional to $n$ and $r$, while being inversely proportional to $N$. On the other hand, the formula for $V(Y)$ is quite opaque, and it is difficult to extract much intuition from it. We will not prove these formulas here. We might prove them once we talk about joint distributions later. Perhaps. If we feel like it.

Problem Prompt.

6. Poisson distributions

We’ve reached our last distribution! (For now.)

Definition. Let $\lambda>0$ be a real number. A random variable $Y$ is said to have a Poisson distribution with parameter $\lambda$, denoted

\begin{equation}\notag Y \sim \Pois(\lambda), \end{equation}

if it has range $\{0,1,2,\ldots\}$ and its probability function $p(y)$ is given by

\begin{equation}\notag p(y) = \frac{\lambda^y}{y!}e^{-\lambda}. \end{equation}

After the craziness of hypergeometric distributions with their three parameters, we are back to considering a one-parameter family. Whew! And the effect of this parameter on the shape of the distributions is clear:

Indeed, increasing the parameter $\lambda$ appears to both increase the mean and variance of the distribution.

The archetypical scenarios for Bernoulli, binomial, and geometric random variables involved coin flips, which can model any sort of repeated scenario with binary and independent outcomes. The archetypical scenario for hypergeometric variables involved selecting balls from an urn. The justifications that each of these scenarios could be modeled by the claimed distributions relied essentially on basic combinatorics. But, as you are about to see, the justification that our archetypical Poisson scenario really can be modeled by a Poisson random variable is of a very different flavor.

An archetypical Poisson scenario.

Q: Over a span of $T$ hours, let $Y$ denote the number of emails that arrive in your inbox. Assuming that the emails arrive at random and independently of each other, and that the mean value of $Y$ is $\lambda$, what is the distribution of $Y$?

A: $Y\sim \Pois(\lambda)$.

To explain, we begin by first deriving an approximation for the distribution of $Y$. To do this, we imagine that the time interval begins at $t=0$, and we visualize the arrivals of emails as marks along the interval $[0,T]$:

The trick now is to chop the time interval $[0,T]$ into small enough subintervals that no more than one email arrives in any given subinterval:

Suppose that there are $n$ subintervals, each of equal length $\Delta t = T/n$:

Now, we let $Z_k$ be the number of emails that arrive in the $k$-th subinterval, for $k=1,2,\ldots,n$. By construction, we have that $Z_k=0$ or $1$, and thus each $Z_k$ is a Bernoulli random variable, parametrized by $p_k$. But what is the parameter $p_k$?

To answer this, recall that we are told the mean value of $Y$ is $\lambda$, and thus the average rate at which the emails arrive should be $r = \lambda / T$. Then, since the length of each of the subintervals is $\Delta t$, we would expect, on average, that exactly

\begin{equation}\notag r \Delta t = \frac{\lambda}{T} \cdot \frac{T}{n} = \frac{\lambda}{n} \end{equation}

emails arrive in the $k$-th subinterval. But we know that

\begin{equation}\notag E(Z_k) = p_k \end{equation}

because $Z_k$ is Bernoulli, and this implies that $p_k = \lambda/n$. Thus

\begin{equation}\notag Z_k \sim \Ber(\lambda/n), \end{equation}

for each $k=1,2,\ldots,n$.

We now bring this all back to $Y$. As long as $n$ is large enough (so that $\Delta t$ is small), we should have

\begin{equation}\label{approx-eqn} Y \approx Z_1 + Z_2 + \cdots + Z_n. \end{equation}

It is natural to assume that the Bernoulli random variables on the right-hand side are independent, which means—as we saw above—that $Y$ is distributed approximately as $\Bin(n,\lambda/n)$. Moreover, it is natural to believe that the approximation \eqref{approx-eqn} gets better and better the larger $n$ is, and that it becomes an equality in the limit as $n\to \infty$.

But a $\Bin(n,\lambda/n)$ random variable has probability function

\begin{equation}\label{func-eqn} p(y) = \binom{n}{y} \left( \frac{\lambda}{n} \right)^y \left(1 - \frac{\lambda}{n} \right)^{n-y} = \left[ \binom{n}{y} \cdot \frac{1}{n^y}\right] \cdot \lambda^y \cdot \left(1 - \frac{\lambda}{n} \right)^{n} \left(1 - \frac{\lambda}{n} \right)^{-y}. \end{equation}

Note that

\begin{equation}\notag \lim_{n\to \infty} \binom{n}{y} \cdot \frac{1}{n^y} = \lim_{n\to \infty} \frac{n}{n}\cdot \frac{n-1}{n} \cdots \frac{n-y+1}{n} \cdot \frac{1}{y!} = \frac{1}{y!} \end{equation}

and

\begin{equation}\notag \lim_{n\to \infty} \left(1 - \frac{\lambda}{n} \right)^{n} = e^{-\lambda}, \end{equation}

as well as

\begin{equation}\notag \lim_{n\to \infty} \left(1 - \frac{\lambda}{n} \right)^{-y} = 1. \end{equation}

Thus, taking the limit $n\to \infty$ in \eqref{func-eqn} gives

\begin{equation}\notag \lim_{n\to \infty} p(y) = \frac{\lambda^y}{y!} e^{-\lambda}, \end{equation}

and so from \eqref{approx-eqn} we conclude that $Y\sim \Pois(\lambda)$.

Expectations and variances of Poisson variables. If $Y\sim \Pois(\lambda)$, then

\begin{equation}\notag E(Y) = \lambda \quad \text{and} \quad V(Y) = \lambda. \end{equation}

Remember, we saw in the probability histograms above that increasing $\lambda$ tends to increase both the center and the spread of the distribution. Well, now we see that this happens because $\lambda$ is both the mean and the variance of the distribution! Proving these formulas isn’t that hard, but as with a few of the previous distributions, we will save the derivations till we have moment-generating functions.

Problem Prompt.
